facts: After the fall of Rome, Western Europe entered the medieval period, a time when there was no single strong central government, so local lords gained power.
Under feudalism, a lord granted land to a vassal (knight) in exchange for military service, while serfs worked the land and were not free to leave.
The Christian Church was the most powerful institution in medieval Europe, influencing both daily life and politics, and it owned large amounts of land.
In 1066, the Normans, led by William the Conqueror, invaded England and defeated the Anglo-Saxons at the Battle of Hastings, bringing Norman rule.
During the Middle Ages, towns grew as trade increased, and many towns gained charters that gave them rights to self-government, unlike the feudal countryside.
In 1215, English nobles forced King John to sign the Magna Carta, which limited the king's power and established the idea that even the ruler must follow the law, a step toward parliament.
misconceptions: Students may think that medieval Europe was a single unified kingdom like the Roman Empire, but it was actually divided into many small territories ruled by local lords.
Students may believe that knights were the only important people in feudalism, but serfs were the majority who worked the land and had no freedom.
Students may think that the Magna Carta gave all English people rights, but it primarily protected the rights of nobles and limited the king's power.
